findings.title: Unsafe `as PersonaEmotion` cast on xstate actor snapshot value
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 6.0). The xstate actor snapshot `.value` is typed as a generic state value. Using `as PersonaEmotion` bypasses type checking entirely -- if the machine definition changes to add new states or use nested states, this cast will silently produce incorrect values at runtime. The same cast appears again on line 194 in `getEmotionState()`. Two occurrences total.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard function: `function assertPersonaEmotion(v: unknown): PersonaEmotion { const valid: Set<string> = new Set(['idle','curious','caring','worried','sleepy','excited']); if (typeof v === 'string' && valid.has(v)) return v as PersonaEmotion; throw new Error('Unexpected emotion state: ' + String(v)); }` -- or configure the xstate machine with typed output so `.value` is properly narrowed without a cast.

findings.title: TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP keyed by plain `string` instead of trigger name union
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 4/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). The map is typed `Record<string, EmotionEvent>` which accepts any arbitrary string key. Since `ALL_TRIGGERS` exports a fixed array of TriggerCondition objects, a literal union type `TriggerName = typeof ALL_TRIGGERS[number]['name']` can be derived and used as the key type. This would catch typos and ensure the map stays in sync with trigger definitions at compile time. Currently, a misspelled key like 'mornng-greeting' would compile without error and silently fail to match at runtime (line 133: `const emotionEvent = TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP[result.triggerName]`).
findings.suggestion: Extract `type TriggerName = typeof ALL_TRIGGERS[number]['name']` from persona-engine exports, then type the map as `Record<TriggerName, EmotionEvent>` or `Partial<Record<TriggerName, EmotionEvent>>` if not all triggers need an explicit emotion mapping.

findings.title: Millisecond config fields use bare `number` without branded types
findings.code_snippet:
screenshotIntervalMs?: number
aggregationIntervalMs?: number
inactivityThresholdMs?: number
eventRetentionMs?: number
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). Four optional config fields represent durations in milliseconds but are typed as plain `number`. Nothing prevents accidentally passing seconds (e.g., `60` instead of `60_000`) or negative values. The `Ms` suffix in the field name is helpful documentation but not enforced by the type system. This is a recurring pattern across context-engine and persona-engine as well.
findings.suggestion: Consider a branded type `type Milliseconds = number & { readonly __brand: 'Milliseconds' }` with a constructor `function ms(value: number): Milliseconds` that validates non-negative. This is a walking-skeleton-level concern so lower priority, but establishing the pattern now prevents drift. Alternatively, add a runtime guard in `createAnimaOrchestrator` that validates all duration fields are positive.

findings.title: AnimaOrchestrator interface + factory function: good encapsulation pattern
findings.code_snippet:
export interface AnimaOrchestrator {
  start(): void
  stop(): void
  recordActivity(event: ActivityEvent): void
  tickScreenshot(): Promise<boolean>
  ...
}
findings.description: Encapsulation: 9/10, Expression: 7/10, Usefulness: 8/10, Enforcement: 7/10 (Aggregate: 7.75). The AnimaOrchestrator uses a public interface with a private factory function (`createAnimaOrchestrator`), effectively hiding the emotionActor, pipeline, monitor, and intimacy state as closure variables. Consumers cannot directly mutate internal state. The `tickScreenshot()` and `tickActivity()` methods enable deterministic testing without timers. This is the recommended Imperative Shell pattern for this codebase.
findings.suggestion: No change needed. This is a positive observation documented as a P3 finding for the record. Consider adding `readonly` to the interface methods if the project adopts strict interface conventions.

positive_observations: AnimaOrchestrator uses interface + factory function pattern, hiding all internal state (emotionActor, pipeline, monitor, intimacy) as closure variables -- excellent Imperative Shell encapsulation
AnimaProactiveEvent marks all fields readonly, preventing accidental mutation of emitted events
TriggerInput and IntimacyState from persona-engine correctly use readonly modifiers on all fields, preserving Functional Core immutability
buildTriggerInput cleanly maps context-engine's ActivityState to persona-engine's TriggerInput, maintaining package boundary separation
Test file uses real sharp-generated PNG buffers and properly documented Test Doubles for platform APIs (ScreenshotProvider, VlmProvider)
